desc: Flame skimmer — the most conspicuous dragonfly in LA County. Males entirely flame-red to orange. Patrols warm ponds, pools, and even swimming pools. Unmistakable.

fm.Body: ♂: flame-orange/red. ♀: golden-brown.
fm.Wings: Broad amber basal wash; increasingly suffused with age
fm.Behavior: Perches on twigs; slow territorial patrol

hp: Patrols ponds, pools, and fountains. Males flame-red, defend territories. Larvae prey on aquatic invertebrates.

desc: Neon skimmer — brighter neon-red than Flame Skimmer with clear wing bases (no amber). Foothill canyon streams. Males vivid red.

fm.Body: ♂: vivid neon-red. ♀: tan-brown.
fm.Behavior: Streamside percher; riffles territory

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Family Libellulidae.

desc: Widow skimmer — striking wing pattern with dark basal patches plus white bands in mature males. Common at ponds. Males defend perches aggressively.

fm.Body: ♂: pruinose blue abdomen. ♀: brown, yellow stripes.
fm.Wings: Dark basal patches; ♂ adds white bands
fm.Behavior: Slow patrol; prominent perches

hp: Pond and lake margins. Males with dark-and-white wing pattern. Perches on tips of vegetation.

desc: Blue dasher — small powder-blue dragonfly found at virtually every pond, pool, and fountain in LA. Males vivid pruinose blue. Extremely common and approachable.

fm.Body: ♂: powder-blue pruinose; striped thorax. ♀: brown.
fm.Behavior: Perches on veg tips; darting flights

hp: At virtually every pond, pool, and fountain in LA. Males powder-blue. Important mosquito control.

desc: Variegated meadowhawk — the most common SoCal meadowhawk. Complex mosaic pattern of red, white, and brown rather than plain red. Common migrant.

fm.Body: ♂: red with gray-brown thorax pattern. ♀: olive-tan.
fm.Wings: Clear; small amber at base sometimes
fm.Behavior: Low perches, rocks; hovers

hp: Migratory — breeds in temporary pools. Complex mosaic wing pattern. Patrols open areas.

desc: Spot-winged glider — breeds in temporary rain pools. Distinguished from Wandering Glider by small but visible hindwing spot. Strong migrant.

fm.Body: Golden-brown; dark dorsal marks
fm.Wings: Small dark brown hindwing base spot
fm.Behavior: Continuous flight; rarely perches; swarms

hp: Breeds in temporary rain pools. Migratory. Patrols open fields. Aquatic larvae develop rapidly.

desc: Wandering glider — the world's most widespread dragonfly, found on every continent except Antarctica. Makes transoceanic migrations tracked by radar. Breeds in rain puddles.

fm.Body: Golden-yellow
fm.Behavior: Perpetual flight; large swarms

hp: Globe skimmer — world's longest insect migration, crossing Indian Ocean (Hobson et al. 2012, PLoS ONE). Breeds in temporary rain pools worldwide.

desc: Black saddlebags — named for the large black patches at the base of each hindwing. Strong migrant. Often seen in feeding swarms high overhead. Common at ponds.

fm.Body: Black abdomen; yellow lateral spots on S7–8
fm.Behavior: Extended flight; migrant swarms

hp: Strong migrant. Breeds at ponds. Black wing patches (saddlebags). Preys on mosquitoes and small insects.

desc: Roseate skimmer — tropical species at its northern range limit in LA County. Mature males uniquely rosy-pink rather than red. Warm ponds.

fm.Body: ♂: rosy-pink. ♀: brown with pale stripe.
fm.Behavior: Aggressive over warm ponds

hp: Breeds at ponds and marshes. Aerial predator of mosquitoes and flies. Aquatic larvae. On Rosa (rose).

desc: Comanche skimmer — pale blue pruinose skimmer of warm desert springs. Distinctively clean white face. Localized at warm-water habitats in desert canyons.

fm.Body: ♂: powder-blue. ♀: brown, yellow stripe.
fm.Wings: Clear; tiny amber at extreme base
fm.Behavior: Perches on boulders near springs

hp: Desert spring/oasis specialist. Breeds at warm-water springs. Aerial predator. Family Libellulidae.

desc: Red rock skimmer — dragonfly of rocky desert streams. Uniquely perches flat against hot boulders with wings spread. Males brick-red. Arid canyon specialists.

fm.Body: ♂: deep red. ♀: tan with dark stripe.
fm.Wings: Clear; red veining at close range
fm.Behavior: Flat on hot rocks; short patrols

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aerial predator. Aquatic larvae in flowing water. Red Rock.

desc: Eight-spotted skimmer — named for four dark wing patches plus four white patches in mature males (eight total). Mountain ponds and lakes. Males territorial over water.

fm.Body: ♂: pruinose blue-white. ♀: brown, yellow stripes.
fm.Behavior: Patrols mountain ponds

hp: Mountain and foothill ponds. Males with eight dark-and-white wing patches. Territorial at water.

desc: Common green darner — among the largest NA dragonflies at 3+ inches. Resident and migratory populations. Distinctive green thorax and blue abdomen. 'Bull's-eye' face mark.

fm.Body: Green thorax; blue abdomen (♂); "bull's-eye" face
fm.Behavior: Tireless hawk; migratory fall swarms

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Largest.

desc: Blue-eyed darner — the most common darner in LA County. Bright blue eyes and blue abdominal spots. Patrols ponds and pools. Strong flier. Nearly year-round.

fm.Body: Blue spots on dark abdomen; blue thoracic stripes
fm.Behavior: Hawks along corridors; hovers frequently

hp: Most common large darner in LA. Patrols pond margins. Hawking predator of flying insects.

desc: Paddle-tailed darner — mountain darner with distinctive paddle-shaped cerci. Forest clearings and mountain ponds. Blue and green mosaic pattern. Late summer flier.

fm.Body: Blue-spotted; blue-green thoracic stripes
fm.Behavior: Hawks through forest clearings

hp: Mountain forest clearings and meadow ponds. Paddle-shaped cerci diagnostic. Hawking predator.

desc: Pacific clubtail — medium gomphid of clear rocky streams. Club-shaped abdominal tip and widely separated eyes diagnostic (most dragonflies have eyes touching). Rests flat on streamside rocks. Western NA endemic. Uncommon — requires clean water.

fm.Body: Black with yellow-green marks; expanded tip (club)
fm.Behavior: Perches on streamside rocks/gravel

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. Flattened body.

desc: Familiar bluet — the most widespread damselfly in North America. The default 'blue damselfly' at any LA County pond. Blue with black dorsal markings.

fm.Body: ♂: blue with black dorsal marks. ♀: variable.
fm.Behavior: Weak flutter near water; emergent perches

hp: Most widespread damselfly in NA. At nearly every water body. Important mosquito larvae predator.

desc: Vivid dancer — striking purple-blue damselfly of rocky streams and thermal hot springs. Unusually cold-tolerant — active even in winter at warm springs.

fm.Body: ♂: vivid blue-violet. ♀: brown with blue stripes.
fm.Behavior: Sunlit rocks; darting flights; tandem pairs
fm.Season: Year-round at thermal sites; Mar–Nov elsewhere

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. Preys on ant. Family Coenagrionidae.

desc: Blue-ringed dancer — pale blue damselfly with distinctive blue ring pattern encircling each abdominal segment. Perches on streamside rocks. Clear flowing streams.

fm.Body: ♂: blue with black rings. ♀: tan.
fm.Behavior: Streamside rocks and vegetation

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. Family Coenagrionidae.

desc: Pacific forktail — small damselfly with four blue thoracic spots (two dorsal, two lateral) and blue-tipped abdomen. The most common damselfly at most LA County ponds and streams. Older females become pruinose gray. Perches on emergent vegetation near water.

fm.Body: ♂: black, green thorax stripes, blue S8–S9. ♀: orange→pruinose.
fm.Behavior: Low perches near still water

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Near water. Most Common.

desc: Black-fronted forktail — all-black dorsal thorax distinguishes this forktail from the Pacific Forktail's green stripes. Common at ponds and slow streams.

fm.Body: ♂: black thorax (no green); blue S8–S10.
fm.Behavior: Similar to Pacific Forktail

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Family Coenagrionidae. Black-fronted.

desc: American rubyspot — spectacular damselfly of foothill streams. Males with brilliant ruby-crimson wing bases that flash in courtship displays. Metallic green-bronze body. Perches on streamside vegetation. One of the most beautiful LA County odonates.

fm.Body: ♂: dark metallic green-bronze. ♀: dull bronze.
fm.Behavior: Slow butterfly-like flight; streamside perches

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. On Fraxinus (ash).

desc: Twelve-spotted skimmer — twelve dark spots on wings (three per wing) are diagnostic. Mature males add eight white patches between the dark spots for a dramatic twenty-spot pattern. Mountain ponds and lakes. Males patrol territories over open water.

fm.Body: ♂: pruinose white abdomen. ♀: brown, yellow stripes.
fm.Wings: 3 dark spots per wing; ♂ adds white patches between
fm.Behavior: Perches on prominent twigs near ponds

hp: Twelve dark wing spots diagnostic. Ponds and lakes. Territorial males perch prominently.

desc: Cardinal meadowhawk — richly red meadowhawk with red wing veining and red face. Common at foothill ponds. Often the most numerous dragonfly at small ponds.

fm.Body: ♂: bright red including face. ♀: golden-brown.
fm.Wings: Red veining in basal area (diagnostic vs. Variegated)
fm.Behavior: Perches low; patrols small ponds

hp: Foothill ponds and streams. Richly red. Preys on flying insects from perches near water.

desc: Striped meadowhawk — pale meadowhawk with prominent white lateral thoracic stripes. Mountain meadows and ponds. Less common than Variegated Meadowhawk.

fm.Body: ♂: red with white thoracic stripes. ♀: yellow-brown.
fm.Behavior: Perches on vegetation near ponds

hp: Mountain meadows and streams. Pale with white thoracic stripes. Higher elevation than other meadowhawks.

desc: Western pondhawk — males are unique powder-blue/green color (no other western dragonfly is this hue). Females bright green. Aggressive territorial predator — attacks dragonflies its own size. Common at ponds. Perches horizontally on low vegetation.

fm.Body: ♂: entirely powder-green pruinose. ♀: bright green with dark markings.
fm.Behavior: Perches low; aggressive; catches large prey

hp: Breeds at ponds and marshes. Aerial predator of mosquitoes and flies. Aquatic larvae.

desc: Mexican amberwing — tiny dragonfly with entirely amber-orange wings in males. One of the smallest NA dragonflies — easily mistaken for a wasp in flight. Females have patterned wings. Warm ponds and streams. Males aggressively territorial despite tiny size.

fm.Body: ♂: orange-amber. ♀: brown with dark marks.
fm.Wings: ♂: entirely amber-orange (diagnostic). ♀: clear with brown spots.
fm.Size: ⅔ in–1 in (very small for dragonfly)
fm.Behavior: Perches on low stems; brief hovering flights

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. Smallest.

desc: Desert whitetail — arid-country skimmer with conspicuous chalky white pruinose abdomen in males. Territorial over warm desert pools. Males raise abdomen in 'obelisk' posture on hot days to thermoregulate. Distinguished from Common Whitetail by range.

fm.Body: ♂: chalk-white pruinose. ♀: brown with pale stripe.
fm.Wings: Dark basal/medial bands (♂ adds white patches)
fm.Behavior: Guards desert pools and springs

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Desert habitat. Family Libellulidae.

desc: Bleached skimmer — pale whitish-pruinose skimmer of warm desert springs. Males become almost entirely white with age. Similar to Comanche Skimmer but face pattern differs. Restricted to desert/semi-arid water sources. Uncommon.

fm.Body: ♂: chalk-white. ♀: brown.
fm.Behavior: Perches on rocks near desert springs

hp: Desert spring/oasis specialist. Breeds at warm-water springs. Aerial predator. Family Libellulidae.

desc: California dancer — delicate blue-and-black damselfly of streams and rivers. Males bright blue. Common along foothill streams. Important indicator of water quality.
hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. Preys on ant. Family Coenagrionidae.

desc: Exclamation damsel — distinctive Pacific Coast damselfly with an exclamation-point-shaped mark on abdominal segment 2. Associated with streams with emergent vegetation. A western NA endemic. Uncommon in LA County — near southern range limit.

fm.Body: ♂: blue with distinctive "!" marking on S2
fm.Behavior: Perches on streamside vegetation

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. Family Coenagrionidae.

desc: Rambur's forktail — distinguished from Pacific and Black-fronted forktails by green thorax combined with orange stigma. Female with extensive orange coloring. Warm ponds and marshes. Originally Neotropical — expanding northward, possibly with climate change.

fm.Body: ♂: green thorax; blue tail tip; orange thoracic stripe in immatures
fm.Behavior: Like other forktails; low vegetation near still water

hp: Ponds and marshes. Green thorax with blue abdomen tip. Important mosquito larvae predator.

desc: Great spreadwing — one of the largest damselflies in North America (body length 50-60mm). Dark bronze-green body with yellow lateral thoracic stripes. Wings held half-spread at rest (unlike most damselflies). Stream edges in foothill canyons.

fm.Body: Dark bronze-green; prominent yellow lateral thoracic stripes
fm.Behavior: Perches in willows and alders along streams

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates.

desc: Swift setwing — dark dragonfly with distinctive wing-forward resting posture (sets wings ahead rather than out to sides). Fast flier around ponds and slow streams. Uncommon in LA.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; wings set forward at rest

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. Family Libellulidae.

desc: Band-winged meadowhawk — distinguished from other meadowhawks by amber band across hindwing. Mountain ponds and marshy areas. Late summer.

fm.Body: ♂: red. ♀: olive-brown.
fm.Wings: Amber band across HW (diagnostic for species)
fm.Behavior: Perches on low vegetation near ponds

hp: Mountain ponds. Amber hindwing band diagnostic. Late-season species — flies into October.

desc: Pale-faced clubskimmer — a fast-flying stream dragonfly with pale face and banded dark abdomen. Hovers over riffles like a helicopter. Distinguished from other stream dragonflies by pale face and hovering behavior. Foothill canyon streams.

fm.Body: Gray-green with dark banding on abdomen; pale face
fm.Wings: Clear with tiny amber basal spot
fm.Behavior: Hovers low over riffles in fast streams

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Near water.

desc: Shadow darner — dark darner of shaded mountain streams. Blue spots on near-black background. Flies late in the day, even into dusk. Montane.

fm.Body: Dark with blue spots; blue thoracic stripes
fm.Behavior: Patrols shaded streams; hovers in forest openings

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Montane.

desc: Gray sanddragon — cryptic clubtail dragonfly of sandy streams. Rests flat on sand and gravel, nearly invisible. Desert and foothill streams. Uncommon.

fm.Body: Gray-brown with darker marks; slightly clubbed
fm.Behavior: Rests flat on sand/gravel — well camouflaged

hp: Desert habitat. Near water. Cryptic camouflage.

desc: Desert forktail — small green-and-black damselfly of desert springs and seeps. Green thoracic stripes. Restricted to scarce desert water sources.

fm.Body: ♂: dark with green stripes; blue tail tip.
fm.Behavior: Low flight near desert springs

hp: Desert spring/oasis specialist. Breeds at warm-water springs. Aerial predator. Family Coenagrionidae.

desc: Filigree skimmer — spectacular dragonfly with intricately patterned brown-and-clear wings. Desert washes and streams. One of the most beautiful NA dragonflies.
hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aerial predator. Aquatic larvae in flowing water. On Fraxinus (ash).

desc: Autumn meadowhawk — the latest-flying meadowhawk, still active into November when most dragonflies have finished. Red face diagnostic. Pairs fly in tandem. Often the last dragonfly species seen each year. Marshy ponds and slow streams.

fm.Body: ♂: red with red face. ♀: olive.
fm.Behavior: Perches on low vegetation; late season specialist

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. Marsh/wetland.

desc: Painted skimmer — amber wing bases and small dark spots create a painted appearance. Uncommon at montane ponds. Less well-known than other Libellula species.

fm.Body: ♂: dark with pale dorsal stripe. ♀: similar.
fm.Wings: Amber bases + small dark patches at nodus and tip
fm.Behavior: Mountain pond margins

hp: Uncommon at mountain ponds. Amber wing bases and small dark spots create painted appearance.

desc: Eastern amberwing — very similar to Mexican Amberwing. Males with entirely amber wings. At extreme western range limit in LA County — primarily an eastern/central NA species. Any LA record is notable. Distinguished from Mexican by subtle venation differences.

fm.Body: ♂: amber-orange. ♀: brown striped.
fm.Wings: ♂: amber throughout. ♀: clear with brown spots.
fm.Behavior: Low stems over warm ponds

hp: Small — often perches on lily pads. Males with entirely amber wings. Warm-water ponds.

desc: Walker's darner — desert darner of warm streams and hot springs. Blue spots on dark body. One of the few darners adapted to arid habitats.

fm.Body: Dark with blue spots; blue lateral thoracic stripes
fm.Behavior: Patrols warm streams and hot springs

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aerial predator. Aquatic larvae in flowing water. Walker's.

desc: Citrine forktail — tiny yellow-green damselfly, one of the smallest in North America. Near warm ponds and marshy edges. Can reproduce parthenogenetically.

fm.Body: ♂: mostly yellow-green (citrine); tiny.
fm.Behavior: Low flight in marshes; easily overlooked

hp: Tiny yellow-green damselfly near warm ponds and marshes. One of the smallest North American odonates.

desc: Fiery-eyed dancer — bronze-colored damselfly with spectacular fiery red eyes in males. Clear rocky streams. Males defend territories on streamside rocks.

fm.Body: ♂: bronze-brown with red eyes. ♀: brown.
fm.Behavior: Rocky stream margins

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. Spectacular.

desc: Emma's dancer — pale blue dancer of warm lowland streams and irrigation channels. Similar to Vivid Dancer but at lower, warmer elevations — the two species often replace each other along an elevational gradient. Named for early collector Emma.

fm.Body: ♂: pale violet-blue with dark marks. ♀: brown.
fm.Behavior: Streamside rocks and vegetation

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Family Coenagrionidae. Emma's.

desc: A dark, sooty-colored dancer of rocky foothill streams. Males nearly black — much darker than other LA County Argia.

fm.Body: ♂: very dark — nearly black with blue highlights on thorax. ♀: dark brown.
fm.Behavior: Rocky stream margins; perches on dark rocks where camouflaged

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Family Coenagrionidae. Sooty.

desc: A tropical dragonfly reaching LA at northern range edge. Perches with wings forward, pennant-style. Red abdomen tip diagnostic.

fm.Body: ♂: brown thorax; abdomen red at tip. ♀: olive-brown throughout.
fm.Wings: Amber at bases; held slightly forward at rest (pennant posture)
fm.Behavior: Perches on tips of vegetation with wings angled forward; warm ponds

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Family Libellulidae.

desc: A large spreadwing endemic to California. Bronze-green with yellow lateral stripes. Wings held half-open (family diagnostic). Associated with willow-lined foothill streams.

fm.Body: ♂: metallic bronze-green; blue pruinose abdomen tip. ♀: bronze-green throughout.
fm.Behavior: Perches on willow branches along seasonal streams; wings half-spread

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aerial predator. Aquatic larvae in flowing water. On Salix (willow).

desc: A large, striking dragonfly of clear mountain streams. Black with yellow markings. Named for the female's spike-like ovipositor used to inject eggs into streambed sediment.

fm.Body: Black with paired yellow dorsal spots on each segment; yellow thoracic stripes
fm.Eyes: Green; meeting at a single point on top of head (diagnostic for family)
fm.Behavior: Patrols up and down streams at low altitude; ♀ hovers vertically, jabbing ovipositor into gravel

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. Montane.

desc: A stream-associated bluet — unlike Familiar Bluet which prefers still water. Blue with distinctive black dorsal abdominal pattern.

fm.Body: ♂: blue with narrow black dorsal marks; black humeral stripe broken. ♀: tan/olive.
fm.Behavior: Stream margins and slow-flowing water; rarely at ponds

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Family Coenagrionidae. Arroyo.

desc: Tule bluet — pond and marsh damselfly often found among tule (bulrush) beds. Blue with wider black dorsal marks than the similar Familiar Bluet. Distinguished by shape of male cerci (requires hand lens). Common at vegetated pond margins.

fm.Body: ♂: blue with broader black dorsal marks than E. civile. ♀: variable.
fm.Behavior: Marsh vegetation, tule beds, pond margins

hp: Pond and marsh damselfly among tule (bulrush) beds. Blue with wide black dorsal abdominal stripe.

desc: A tiny, brilliant red damselfly of warm ponds and desert springs. One of the smallest and most colorful LA County odonates.

fm.Body: ♂: entirely bright red-orange (diagnostic). ♀: olive-tan.
fm.Behavior: Low flight among emergent vegetation at warm ponds; desert springs

hp: Breeds at ponds and marshes. Aerial predator of mosquitoes and flies. Aquatic larvae. Preys on ant.

desc: A distinctive clubtail with grapple-hook-shaped cerci (male). Found at clear foothill streams. Separated eyes (Gomphidae diagnostic).

fm.Body: Black with yellow markings; ♂ cerci curved like grappling hooks (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Perches on streamside rocks and gravel bars

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. On apple.

desc: Lavender dancer — pale lavender-blue dancer of warm streams and desert springs. More violet-toned than the similar Vivid Dancer. Males patrol territories over riffles. Named for entomologist James Hine. Desert and foothill streams.

fm.Body: ♂: pale lavender-blue with dark markings. ♀: tan with pale stripes.
fm.Behavior: Warm stream margins; often syntopic with A. vivida

hp: Lavender dancer — pale lavender-blue damselfly of warm desert streams and springs. Aquatic larvae in flowing warm water. Desert oasis indicator.

desc: Named for its serpentine color pattern — green thorax with dark bands resembling a kingsnake. A clubtail of rocky streams.

fm.Body: Green thorax with dark lateral bands (serpent-like); yellow-green abdomen
fm.Behavior: Perches on streamside boulders; wary and difficult to approach

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Family Gomphidae.

desc: Males with chalk-white pruinose abdomen and dark wing bands. A widespread eastern species at western range edge in LA County — rare.

fm.Body: ♂: chalk-white pruinose abdomen. ♀: brown with pale dorsal stripe.
fm.Wings: ♂: dark basal and medial bands. ♀: 3 dark spots per wing.
fm.Behavior: Perches on ground or low objects near ponds

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Rare.

desc: The largest dragonfly in North America — wingspan can exceed 5 in. Green thorax; blue abdomen. Patrols over streams and pools.

fm.Body: Green thorax; bright blue abdomen (♂); similar to A. junius but much larger
fm.Behavior: Powerful patrol flight along streams; hovers; takes large prey

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Near water. Size: 5 in.

desc: The smallest NA forktail. Green thorax and blue tail tip — similar to Pacific Forktail but significantly smaller.

fm.Body: ♂: tiny; green thorax stripes; blue S8–S9. ♀: orange immature → pruinose.
fm.Behavior: Low vegetation near still water; easily overlooked due to size

hp: Smallest NA forktail damselfly. Near ponds and slow streams. Preys on tiny flying insects.

desc: Great pondhawk — large bright green tropical dragonfly. Bigger and more robust than the common Western Pondhawk. Extremely rare vagrant to SoCal from Mexico. Any LA County record is a major rarity event and should be thoroughly documented.

fm.Body: ♂: bright green (larger than E. collocata). ♀: green with dark markings.
fm.Size: 2–2.5 in; noticeably larger than Western Pondhawk
fm.Behavior: Like Western Pondhawk but larger; tropical vagrant

hp: Large green tropical dragonfly. At warm ponds. More robust than Western Pondhawk. At range edge.

desc: Aztec dancer — violet-blue dancer of warm desert streams. At extreme western range edge in LA County — primarily a species of the American Southwest and Mexico. Any LA sighting represents a range-edge record. On warm, slow streams.

fm.Body: ♂: violet-blue with dark marks. ♀: brown.
fm.Behavior: Warm desert stream margins

hp: Aztec dancer — violet-blue damselfly of warm desert streams. Breeds at springs and seeps. Aquatic larvae. Desert riparian specialist.

desc: A medium-sized spreadwing with conspicuous dark stigma. Wings held half-open. Single LA County obs— likely stray from mountain or northern populations.

fm.Body: ♂: metallic bronze-green with blue pruinose tip. ♀: bronze.
fm.Wings: Clear; conspicuous dark stigma; held half-open
fm.Behavior: Marsh and pond vegetation; wings half-spread

hp: Spreadwing — wings held half-open at rest. Near marshes and ponds. Rare in LA County.

desc: Western river cruiser — large fast-flying dragonfly patrolling rivers with a characteristic undulating flight path. Brilliant green eyes. Rarely perches — hunts on the wing. Brown body with yellow abdominal rings. One of the most impressive stream dragonflies.

fm.Color: Dark with green eyes; yellow spots
fm.Flight: Fast patrolling over rivers — rarely perches

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aerial predator. Aquatic larvae in flowing water. Preys on ant.

desc: Dot-tailed whiteface — small black dragonfly with bright yellow spot on abdominal segment 7 (diagnostic). White face. Bog and pond specialist. At extreme southern range limit in LA County mountains — primarily a boreal species.

fm.Color: Black with bright yellow spot on S7
fm.Habitat: Bogs, marshy ponds — southern range limit

hp: Small black dragonfly with yellow tail spot. Mountain ponds. Preys on small flying insects.

desc: Canyon rubyspot — metallic-bodied damselfly with red wing bases similar to American Rubyspot but found in deeper, more shaded canyon streams. Males territorial. Legs used to clasp perches in fast water. One of the gems of foothill stream surveys.
hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. Metallic.

desc: Crimson-ringed whiteface — dark dragonfly with red abdominal rings and white face. Mountain ponds and bogs. A boreal species at extreme southern range limit in LA County's highest elevations. Any San Gabriel Mountains record is biogeographically significant.
hp: Mountain ponds. Dark with red abdominal rings and white face. Higher elevation specialist.

desc: Blue-fronted dancer — blue damselfly with distinctive dark wing tips (apical spots). Streams and rivers in foothill canyons. Males patrol territories over riffles. Distinguished from other Argia by darkened wing tips.

fm.Habitat: Streams and rivers in foothill canyons

hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aquatic larvae prey on invertebrates. Family Coenagrionidae.

desc: Cherry-faced meadowhawk — red meadowhawk with distinctive solid cherry-red face (other meadowhawks have striped or pale faces). Marshy mountain ponds. Late season — often the last meadowhawk flying. Distinguished from Variegated by solid red face.
hp: Mountain meadow ponds. Red with solid cherry-red face — distinguishes from other meadowhawks.

desc: Paiute dancer — small pale blue damselfly of desert springs and alkaline streams. A regional specialty of the Great Basin — at western range limit in LA County. Distinguished from other blue dancers by habitat (alkaline/desert springs) and subtle pattern differences.
hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aerial predator. Aquatic larvae in flowing water. Paiute.

desc: Kiowa dancer — dark dancer of slow desert streams, irrigation ditches, and cattle tanks. Males very dark blue-black. Desert lowlands. Named for the Kiowa people. At western range edge in LA County.
hp: Breeds at streams and rivers. Aerial predator. Aquatic larvae in flowing water. Kiowa.

desc: Red saddlebags — large dragonfly with bright red basal wing patches (the 'saddlebags'). Strong migrant — appears unpredictably at ponds. Hovers and glides over open water.

fm.Color: Red body; red basal wing patches ('saddlebags')
fm.Behavior: Strong migrant — appears unpredictably

hp: Strong migrant. Red wing base patches (saddlebags). At ponds and lakes. Preys on flying insects.
